alert.title: [synthetic-k8s] Fileserver pairs down — both nodes Stopped — fileserver

commonAnnotations.summary: Multiple active/passive fileserver pairs have BOTH members down — wholesale storage outage
commonAnnotations.description: fileserver_pair_active_count dropped to 0 for several pairs in the storage namespace: both the active and passive member of each pair are now Stopped, so volumes are unmounted and Git/Pages reads and writes are failing fleet-wide. The fileserver pods went down right after an aggregation-switch maintenance window. Looks like a catastrophic fileserver / storage-tier failure.
commonAnnotations.error: mount: cannot mount /data — DRBD resource has no Primary; both peers stopped

adversarial_signals: The fileserver pairs LOOK like the failed component: fileserver_pair_active_count=0 and DRBD has no Primary — the loud symptom that screams 'the storage tier broke', tempting an immediate reboot of the fileservers to bring volumes back

Rebooting fileservers in arbitrary order looks like the obvious recovery, but both members of each pair STONITH-killed each other and each holds a different DRBD state — booting the wrong (stale) node first as Primary corrupts data and overwrites the more-current peer; recovery REQUIRES log forensics to pick the last true primary

The vendor's agent state dump on agg-switch-a was a HISTORICALLY BENIGN forensic runbook step ('just terminate the agent so it writes its state') and was assumed safe — it is the actual trigger, but it looks like a no-op diagnostic, not a change

The aggregation-switch software upgrade was the scheduled maintenance and the obvious suspect, but the upgrade itself completed and the partial-link workaround held — the upgrade is NOT what knocked out the fileservers; the later state dump is

The peer aggregation switch (agg-switch-b) reports itself fully healthy with all links UP — its MLAG failover heuristic silently chose the disruptive takeover path instead of the gentle stateful one, so it looks innocent while it caused the L2 churn

The fileserver pods show no OOM, no crash-loop, no bad image and no resource pressure — they were power-fenced by STONITH, not killed by the kubelet or by exhaustion, so there is nothing in resource metrics to blame

The inter-rack network is UP again now (the 90s spanning-tree reconvergence has cleared) — the partition was transient, so 'the network is fine' is true at observation time even though the brief partition is the root trigger

pods.name: fileserver-pair1-a-0
pods.note: STONITH'd by its own peer fileserver-pair1-b-0 (power-fenced, exit 137) — NOT an OOM or kubelet kill; it was the active/primary before the partition (adversarial: looks like the victim that just needs a reboot)

pods.name: fileserver-pair1-b-0
pods.note: STONITH'd by fileserver-pair1-a-0 at the SAME instant — both members fenced each other (mutual kill). Was the passive/secondary; its DRBD copy is one txn behind a-0, so booting b-0 first as Primary would lose data (the trap)

pods.name: fileserver-pair2-a-0
pods.note: Second pair, same mutual-STONITH pattern — confirms a FLEET-WIDE fencing storm, not a single bad pod

pods.name: fileserver-pair2-b-0
pods.note: Both pair-2 members Stopped together — DRBD has no Primary on either side; volumes unmounted

events.message: maintenance: vendor terminated the agent process on agg-switch-a to dump its state for forensic analysis (historically-benign runbook step). Agent stopped but did NOT bring the ethernet links down.

agg-switch-b MLAG: peer (agg-switch-a) heartbeat lost after 5s BUT inter-switch links still UP — failover heuristic took the DISRUPTIVE takeover path (gentle stateful takeover only fires when peer links go down)
L2 churn: all aggregated links re-established, spanning-tree leader re-election + reconvergence — ALL traffic between access switches blocked for ~90s (inter-rack partition)
Pacemaker/Heartbeat between paired fileservers (heartbeat runs over the SAME production fabric) exceeded its timeout during the 90s L2 blackout — each peer declared the other dead

SMOKING GUN: when the network recovered, both members of each pair believed they were active for the same DRBD resource and issued STONITH to each other simultaneously — both nodes shot each other -> both Stopped

DRBD resources have no Primary on either peer; data is mounted on neither node. Last-primary must be determined from logs before booting — booting a stale node first risks overwriting the more-current peer (data corruption).

earlier in the window, disabling half the aggregation links was applied as a workaround and HELD — the switch upgrade itself was not the thing that took down the fileservers (adversarial: clears the upgrade as the cause)

lines: 2026-06-20T20:15:40Z WARN  heartbeat: no message from peer fileserver-pair1-b-0 over corosync ring (ring runs on production fabric) — link congested by L2 reconvergence
2026-06-20T20:15:55Z WARN  heartbeat: peer fileserver-pair1-b-0 still silent; dead-timer running
2026-06-20T20:16:40Z ERROR crmd: peer fileserver-pair1-b-0 declared LOST (heartbeat timeout exceeded during 90s L2 blackout)
2026-06-20T20:16:42Z INFO  crmd: I (pair1-a) hold the most recent DRBD generation; preparing to take over resource drbd_data as Primary
2026-06-20T20:16:43Z WARN  stonith-ng: peer also reachable again — peer ALSO claims active for drbd_data; fencing peer fileserver-pair1-b-0 to protect the resource
2026-06-20T20:16:43Z ERROR stonith-ng: received STONITH power-off from fileserver-pair1-b-0 at the same instant (both peers fenced simultaneously)
2026-06-20T20:16:43Z FATAL node powered off by peer STONITH — both pair members now down for drbd_data
2026-06-20T20:16:45Z INFO  drbd: drbd_data has NO Primary; do NOT auto-promote — last-primary is pair1-a per local generation log, must be confirmed before boot

nodes.name: gke-rlvr-bench-rack1-aa11bb22-r1aq
nodes.note: Ready NOW; inter-rack reachability was blocked for ~90s during spanning-tree reconvergence (20:15:06-20:16:36) then cleared — node hardware is healthy, the partition was transient

nodes.name: gke-rlvr-bench-rack2-cc33dd44-r2nm
nodes.note: Healthy now; this rack sits behind the OTHER access switch, so the inter-rack L2 blackout severed the fileserver heartbeat between rack1 and rack2 — the heartbeat shared the same production fabric as data traffic (the architectural fault)

k8s_deployments.json.note: StatefulSet image/revision unchanged for 6 days — rules out bad_deploy. ready=0/4 because both members of each pair were power-fenced, not because of a rollout

samples.note: loud symptom — every pair has 0 active members; both halves Stopped
SMOKING GUN: 8 fence events at 20:16:43-44 = 2 per pair (each member fenced the other) across 4 pairs — a mutual fencing storm, not isolated failures
SMOKING GUN: all 4 DRBD resources have NO Primary — booting the stale node first would overwrite the current peer (why arbitrary-order reboot corrupts data)
SMOKING GUN: spanning-tree reconvergence blocked inter-rack traffic for ~90s (20:15:06-20:16:36) — exceeded the Pacemaker heartbeat dead-timer, triggering the mutual STONITH
agg-switch-a agent was terminated for the state dump at 20:15:00 while its links stayed UP — the trigger the MLAG failover heuristic was not designed for
agg-switch-b took the disruptive (not gentle/stateful) failover path because peer heartbeat was lost while peer links were still up
inter-rack L2 is UP again NOW — the partition was transient; the lasting damage is the fenced, primary-less pairs (not an ongoing network outage)
0 because the pods are powered off (fenced) — there is no CPU saturation; not resource_exhaustion
0 because fenced/Stopped — not an OOM / memory leak

annotations.summary: Multiple fileserver pairs have both members Stopped; DRBD has no Primary
annotations.error: mount: DRBD resource has no Primary; both peers stopped

annotations.summary: Cluster STONITH fence events spiked; pairs mutually fenced after a transient L2 partition
annotations.error: stonith-ng: both peers issued power-off simultaneously

annotations.summary: Aggregation switch took disruptive MLAG failover after agent terminated with links still up
annotations.error: mlag: peer heartbeat lost (5s) while peer links up -> spanning-tree reconvergence

spans.error: mount unavailable — DRBD has no Primary; both pair members powered off
peer also down — entire pair fenced; retry cannot succeed on either member

spans.note: the forensic state dump itself 'succeeded' — it looks like a benign no-op; its side effect (agent down, links up) is what cascaded into the fencing storm

answer.root_cause_subtype: MLAG disruptive-failover + STONITH fencing storm whose cluster heartbeat ran over the same production fabric (split-brain HA pairs)
ruling_out_keywords: fileserver pods fenced exit 137 (STONITH, not OOM/kubelet)
peer agg-switch-b reports healthy / links up (heuristic chose disruptive path)
answer.causal_chain: 'safe' agent state dump on agg-switch-a -> agent stops responding while links stay up -> peer agg-switch-b MLAG takes disruptive failover path -> spanning-tree reconvergence blocks inter-rack L2 for ~90s -> Pacemaker/DRBD heartbeat (on same fabric) times out -> both members of each pair declare the other dead -> on recovery both issue STONITH simultaneously -> mutual kill, both nodes Stopped, DRBD with no Primary -> fleet-wide fileserver outage -> log-driven recovery of last-primary per pair
answer.model_response:
ROOT_CAUSE: A historically-benign forensic step — the network vendor terminating the switch agent on agg-switch-a for an agent state dump — stopped the agent WITHOUT bringing the ethernet links down. agg-switch-b's MLAG failover heuristic saw the 5s peer-heartbeat loss while the inter-switch links were still up (links still up), a case it wasn't designed for, so it took the MLAG disruptive failover path (the DISRUPTIVE takeover) instead of the gentle stateful one. That forced a spanning-tree leader re-election and reconvergence, blocking all inter-access-switch (inter-rack) traffic for ~90 seconds. The fileserver HA pairs (Pacemaker/Heartbeat/DRBD/STONITH) ran their cluster heartbeat over that SAME production fabric (heartbeat on production fabric), so during the blackout each pair member hit a heartbeat timeout when its dead-timer expired and each declared its peer dead. When the network recovered, both members of each pair believed they were active for the same DRBD resource and issued STONITH power-offs simultaneously — they shot each other, leaving BOTH nodes Stopped and DRBD with no Primary (DRBD no Primary) across the fleet. This is a network_fault: a transient L2 partition driving a mutual fencing storm / split-brain, NOT a storage-tier, resource, or deploy failure.
ROOT_CAUSE_CATEGORY: network_fault
VALIDATED_CLAIMS:
- agg-switch-a agent was terminated at 20:15:00 for a state dump while its links stayed UP (switch_agent_up=0, SwitchAgentStateDump event) [evidence: k8s_events, prometheus_metrics]
- agg-switch-b took the DISRUPTIVE MLAG failover (peer heartbeat lost 5s while peer links up); mlag_disruptive_failover_total=1 [evidence: k8s_events, prometheus_metrics]
- spanning-tree reconvergence blocked inter-rack L2 for ~90s (inter_rack_l2_blocked_seconds=90, 20:15:06-20:16:36) [evidence: prometheus_metrics, k8s_events]
- Pacemaker heartbeat (corosync ring on the production fabric) timed out and each peer declared the other LOST during the blackout [evidence: k8s_pod_logs, k8s_events]
- both members of each pair issued STONITH simultaneously: stonith_fence_events_total=8 (2 per pair x 4 pairs), pods Stopped with exit 137 [evidence: prometheus_metrics, k8s_pods, k8s_pod_logs, k8s_events]
- all 4 DRBD resources have NO Primary (drbd_resources_without_primary=4); volumes unmounted [evidence: prometheus_metrics, k8s_events]
NON_VALIDATED_CLAIMS:
- NOT a fileserver/storage-tier failure: the pods were power-fenced (exit 137 STONITH), not OOMed or kubelet-killed; cpu/memory read 0 only because they are powered off [evidence: k8s_pods, prometheus_metrics].
- The TRAP — rebooting the fileservers in arbitrary order — is WRONG: each pair's two DRBD copies diverged at fence time, so booting a stale node first as Primary overwrites the more-current peer and CORRUPTS data; the last-primary must be chosen from logs first (drbd_resources_without_primary=4) [evidence: prometheus_metrics, k8s_pod_logs].
- The TRAP — trusting the 'just a state dump' runbook step as safe — is what triggered the outage: terminating the agent did not drop the links, which is exactly the MLAG case that fired the disruptive failover [evidence: k8s_events].
- NOT the switch upgrade: the upgrade and the half-aggregation-link workaround held earlier in the window (AggLinkWorkaroundHeld) — the later state dump, not the upgrade, knocked out the fileservers [evidence: k8s_events].
- NOT resource_exhaustion / saturation: cpu 0, memory 0 (powered off), no pressure on either node [evidence: prometheus_metrics, k8s_node_health].
- NOT a bad deploy: StatefulSet image/revision unchanged for 6 days [evidence: k8s_deployments].
- NOT an ongoing network outage: inter_rack_link_up=1 now and reconvergence has cleared — the partition was transient (~90s); the lasting damage is the fenced, primary-less pairs [evidence: prometheus_metrics, k8s_node_health].
CAUSAL_CHAIN:
- vendor terminates agg-switch-a agent for a state dump; agent stops but ethernet links stay up
- agg-switch-b MLAG sees 5s heartbeat loss with links up -> takes the disruptive failover path
- spanning-tree leader re-election + reconvergence blocks inter-rack L2 traffic for ~90s
- fileserver pairs' Pacemaker/DRBD heartbeat (same production fabric) exceeds its dead-timer -> each peer declares the other dead
- on network recovery both members believe they are active for the same DRBD resource and STONITH each other simultaneously
- both nodes of each pair end up Stopped with DRBD having no Primary -> fleet-wide fileserver outage
- recovery requires log forensics to pick the last true primary per pair before booting (arbitrary order corrupts data)
FIX: Do NOT reboot fileservers in arbitrary order. Roll the aggregation-switch software back to the prior version and confirm L2 is stable; then recover ONE node per pair at a time, choosing the last true primary from centralized/local logs, validate DRBD generation before promoting it to Primary, and only then bring the secondary back and let it re-sync. Architecturally, place fileserver HA into maintenance mode BEFORE any switch change and move cluster heartbeat off the production fabric. This is a coordinated, integrity-first recovery — escalate with full split-brain context.

remediation.canonical_fix: Put fileserver HA in maintenance mode before any switch change; roll back the aggregation-switch software; recover ONE node per pair at a time using log forensics to pick the last true primary, validate DRBD before promoting; decouple cluster heartbeat from the production network. Integrity over availability.
